@graph.name: Mercedes Sprinter 211-317 CDI Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes Sprinter 211–317 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Sprinter diesel engine typically costs £3,500–£6,500 supply only depending on which engine you have. Used OM651 engines are available from approximately £2,500–£4,500. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines can exceed £11,000.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes Sprinter 211–317 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The Sprinter's diesel engines are fundamentally reliable — 200,000–300,000 km examples of the OM651 are common. However, each engine has known weak points: the OM651 suffers from timing chain stretch from ~120,000 km, early Delphi injector failures, and EGR clogging. The OM642 V6 is prone to injector seal failure ('black death'). The OM654 has AdBlue/SCR system sensitivities. All three engines reward correct diesel-spec oil and proper maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes Sprinter 211–317 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues vary by engine. OM651: Timing chain stretch (rattling on cold start, cam/crank codes), early Delphi piezo injector failures, EGR clogging, turbocharger failure, and water pump leaks. OM642: Injector seal failure ('black death' — chuffing noise, soot around injectors), EGR faults, and oil leaks. OM654: AdBlue/SCR system faults (crystallization, NOx sensor failures), and DPF issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes Sprinter engine, or should I scrap the van?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and commercial use. Against a well-kept £20,000 Sprinter, a £5,000 recon is 25% — worth it for a commercial asset. Against a tired £5,000 example, the same recon approaches our 70% Mercedes threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Well-maintained Sprinters retain strong commercial value — a proper engine replacement is often justified for a clean example.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes Sprinter engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. W906 Sprinter (2006–2018) 2.1 CDI: OM651. W906 Sprinter (2006–2018) 3.0 CDI: OM642. VS30 Sprinter (2019–present): OM654. The badge on the front grille or tailgate is the easiest tell — '211 CDI', '311 CDI', '317 CDI', etc.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the timing chain on the OM651, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative timing chain, guide, and tensioner replacement costs £800–£2,500. The OM651's timing chain is its single most critical failure point — it drives both camshafts and the high-pressure fuel pump. If it stretches beyond tolerance or a guide fails, the consequences range from misfires and cam/crank correlation codes to catastrophic engine damage if the chain jumps a tooth. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes using MB 229.51-approved oil are the single most effective preventative measure.

eraMap.title: The SPRINTER 211–317 CDI THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: The workhorse four-cylinder. 95–190 hp. Timing chain stretch and early Delphi injectors are the main concerns.
The torquey V6 — 190 hp, 440 Nm. Smooth and strong, but injector seal failure ("black death") and EGR issues are known patterns.
The current-generation 2.0-litre — 170 hp, 400 Nm. Aluminium block with integrated emissions systems. More refined, but AdBlue/SCR faults are a concern.

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes Sprinter 211–317 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the Sprinter diesel engine family. Reconditioned Mercedes Sprinter 211 CDI engines are available from specialist suppliers. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average, with Mercedes engine replacements typically around £3,500 or more. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only. Main dealer new engines can exceed £11,000.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Sprinter engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM642 V6 requires more labour than the four-cylinder engines.

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch & Tensioner Wear — OM651
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the engine, cam/crank correlation fault codes, misfires, eventual catastrophic failure if ignored.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,500 (chain + tensioner + guide replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £3,500–£7,500 (if chain snaps and valves strike pistons)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The OM651's timing chain is its single most critical failure point. The timing chain drives both camshafts and the high-pressure fuel pump — if it stretches beyond tolerance or a guide fails, the consequences range from misfires and cam/crank correlation codes to catastrophic engine damage if the chain jumps a tooth. In one documented case, a timing chain stretched beyond repair, leading to valve timing issues and eventual seizure. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain and tensioners inspected immediately. Regular oil changes using MB 229.51-approved oil are the single most effective preventative measure.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Injector Seal Failure ("Black Death") — OM642
problems.symptoms: Chuffing or ticking noise from the engine, smell of exhaust gases inside the cabin, soot buildup around injectors, rough running, loss of power, hard cold starts.
problems.repairCost: £600–£1,500 (injector seal replacement — one or more injectors)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £4,500–£9,500 (if left untreated and damages the cylinder head)
problems.recommendation: "Black death" is the most notorious issue on the OM642 V6. When injector seals fail, degraded copper injector seals leak combustion gases, carbonizing into a hard, black crust. If left untreated, the injector can seize in the cylinder head, requiring cylinder head removal or replacement. The OM642's classic V6 weak point is injector seal seepage. If you notice a chuffing noise or smell of exhaust gases inside the cabin, have the injector seals inspected immediately. Replace injector seals every 150,000 miles (240,000 km) as preventative maintenance.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector seal failure guide

problems.title: EGR & DPF/AdBlue System Faults — All Engines
problems.symptoms: Hesitation under acceleration, loss of power, limp mode, DPF warning light, "AdBlue Fault" warning, increased fuel consumption, poor throttle response.
problems.repairCost: £300–£1,200 (EGR cleaning or replacement; DPF clean or regeneration)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £3,500–£9,500 (if emissions system damage leads to engine issues)
problems.recommendation: Under typical UK driving conditions (short trips, traffic, low-load operation), soot builds inside the intake and EGR passages. EGR flow becomes inconsistent, and the ECU compares requested vs measured air mass — airflow deviation triggers torque reduction. This is felt as sluggish acceleration, sudden power drops, and limp mode. On Euro 6 OM651 variants with SCR/AdBlue systems, EGR instability may interact with NOx control strategies, making faults appear more complex. If you predominantly drive in town, ensure the vehicle completes regular regeneration cycles — a motorway run at 2,000+ rpm for 20 minutes at least once a week is recommended. Use only ISO 22241-certified DEF fluid for AdBlue systems.

failureLink.label: Read the full EGR & DPF failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Sprinter 211–317 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Cleaning or replacement — cheaper than engine replacement
Regular regeneration and quality fluid prevent issues
Typically requires full engine replacement or complete rebuild

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Sprinter? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with evidence of regular oil changes using MB 229.51-approved oil
* Timing chain inspected or replaced (OM651 — especially on higher-mileage examples)
* Emissions system regularly maintained (regular motorway runs, quality AdBlue)
* No cold-start rattle, loss of power, or emissions warnings
avoidIf: * Cold-start rattle with no timing chain history (OM651)
* Chuffing noise or exhaust smell in the cabin (OM642 — "black death")
* DPF or AdBlue warning lights with no documented repair
* Poor service history — the Sprinter's commercial engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes Sprinter 211–317 CDI range represents the backbone of the UK commercial van fleet — powered by Mercedes' proven diesel engine family. The OM651 four-cylinder is a workhorse with known timing chain and injector vulnerabilities. The OM642 V6 is smooth and torquey but suffers from injector seal "black death." The current OM654 is more refined but has AdBlue/SCR system sensitivities. All three engines are fundamentally reliable when properly maintained — but they demand diligent servicing, quality oil, and regular emissions system care. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained Sprinter is a £10,000–£35,000 commercial asset; a neglected one is a £5,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare Sprinter engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes Sprinter 211–317 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or ticking (OM651: timing chain; OM642: injector seal)
* Service History — including which engine era (OM651/OM642/OM654) and confirmed oil-change intervals using MB 229.51-approved oil
* Injector Seal Check (OM642) — Check for soot around injectors or chuffing noise ("black death" warning)
* DPF/AdBlue Check — Any DPF or AdBlue warning lights or regeneration issues? (urban-driven vehicles are high risk)
* Turbo Check — Any whistling, grinding, or loss of boost? (turbo failure warning)
* Smoke Check — Blue/white smoke can indicate turbo or injector issues; black smoke can indicate EGR or DPF issues
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, injector, EGR, DPF, or turbo codes?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.

items.label: * OM651
items.value: Displacement: 2,143 cc · Cylinders: Inline-4, DOHC, 16 valves · Bore x Stroke: 83 × 99 mm · Compression Ratio: ~16.2:1 · Injection: Common-rail direct injection (CDI) · Turbo: Single or twin-turbo (variant-dependent)

items.label: * OM642
items.value: Displacement: 2,987 cc · Cylinders: V6, 72° configuration, 24 valves · Block: Aluminium/aluminium

items.label: * OM654
items.value: Displacement: 1,950 cc · Cylinders: Inline-4, 4 valves per cylinder · Block: Aluminium with integrated DPF/SCR aftertreatment
